concentration.headline: Primate city dominance — one city overwhelms all others
concentration.body: Nairobi accounts for a striking 44.9% of tracked urban population across 80 cities in Kenya. This level of primacy (45%) is characteristic of rapidly urbanising or historically centralised economies where one city concentrates political, economic and cultural power.

gravity.headline: Dense gravitational cluster — Nairobi & Kikuyu
gravity.body: The strongest gravitational pull exists between Nairobi and Kikuyu (18 km apart, combined population 5,868,881). Cities form a tight gravitational cluster with top pairs averaging 16 km apart. This dense sub-regional network often functions as a single megalopolitan economy.

narration.synthesis: Kenya exhibits strong primate city dominance: Nairobi holds 44.9% of tracked urban population — a striking concentration. The gravity network confirms this: connections involving Nairobi dominate, with Nairobi → Kikuyu among secondary links. This pattern is characteristic of historically centralised or rapidly urbanising economies.
